pair_convention.spot_explanation: CAD/JPY at 113.81 means 1 Canadian Dollar buys 113.81 Japanese Yen. A rising CAD/JPY rate indicates the Canadian Dollar is strengthening against the Japanese Yen (each CAD buys more JPY), while a falling CAD/JPY rate indicates the Canadian Dollar is weakening against the Japanese Yen (each CAD buys fewer JPY). All bullish and bearish calls in this report are expressed relative to this convention.

executive_summary: CAD/JPY trades at 113.81, having retreated from its 30 April cycle high of 117.49 to give back ground across every horizon: -1.49% over 1M, -1.34% over 3M and -0.98% over 6M, while still up a robust 6.77% over 1Y. The pair sits at the 77th percentile of its 52-week range (101.25–117.49), below all three major moving averages (50d 115.08, 100d 115.38, 200d 114.53) — a structure that has flipped from clean uptrend to early topping. The dominant bilateral narrative is a collision of two crowded shorts pointing in opposite directions: speculators are extreme short CAD (negative carry, oil collapse) AND record-extreme short JPY (carry funding), which mutes the net positioning signal but means a generalized risk-off carry unwind would punish CAD/JPY violently from both legs simultaneously. The pair's defining structural feature is its dual identity — a commodity-vs-safe-haven, oil-correlated carry barometer — that makes it one of the most risk-sensitive crosses in G10. The bilateral rate differential has compressed dramatically: CAD now out-yields JPY by only +125bp (BoC 2.25% vs BoJ 1.00%), down from far wider historical levels and shrinking as the BoJ hikes while the BoC sits at the bottom of neutral. The June oil collapse (WTI ~$75.5) is doubly bearish for CAD/JPY — it strips CAD's petro-bid AND improves Japan's terms of trade. Net: tactically mildly bearish CAD/JPY (low conviction, the pair grinds lower as carry compresses and oil weighs), turning to a more decisive bearish-CAD/JPY strategic call as BoJ normalization, JPY's structural undervaluation and the narrowing differential dominate — with the caveat that timing is hostage to the risk regime and the two crowded shorts can extend before resolving.

market_context.valuation_assessment: On a bilateral PPP/REER basis the pair is structurally rich. The CAD dossier flags CAD as very rich vs deeply undervalued JPY — Canada's REER at 96.58 versus Japan's at just 65.70 — implying CAD is roughly 47% overvalued against JPY on a real trade-weighted basis. The JPY dossier independently rates the yen as the most fundamentally undervalued G10 currency at a REER near the 4th percentile of its multi-decade range, with USD/JPY PPP near 100-110. The two views reinforce: CAD/JPY sits far above any plausible fair value, and the entirety of that gap is explained by the rate/carry differential rather than any fundamental CAD strength. This is the core strategic short-CAD/JPY case — a deeply cheap quote currency that is now normalizing.

market_context.recent_regime: The pair's recent behaviour is a textbook risk-and-oil story. CAD/JPY rallied to a 30 April cycle high of 117.49 on the Middle East war oil spike (Canada reaping rewards from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz while Japan suffered capital outflows to pay for hyper-inflated energy imports), then reversed hard as the 15 June US-Iran deal collapsed oil and that exact terms-of-trade thesis flipped 180 degrees. The pair now grinds lower in a 113–116 band, below its moving averages, with the dominant narrative being carry-differential compression plus the loss of CAD's oil bid against a normalizing BoJ.

bilateral_carry_analysis.breakeven_move: At +130bp annualised, a long CAD/JPY earns ~10.8bp/month, equivalent to ~+12 pips/month of spot cushion from 113.81. A long carry position is wiped out by a spot fall of just ~0.4% over a quarter — trivially small versus the pair's 9-11% vol, meaning spot risk dwarfs carry income.
bilateral_carry_analysis.carry_assessment: CAD/JPY remains a positive-carry long for CAD holders, but the cushion has thinned dramatically. The bilateral policy differential is +125bp (BoC 2.25% vs BoJ 1.00%), with the 10Y bilateral spread wider at +171bp (Canada 3.46% vs Japan ~1.75%), providing modestly more generous term carry. The carry-to-vol ratio of only ~0.13 is far weaker than comparable commodity-cross carry trades such as AUD/JPY (+335bp / ~0.30+), meaning CAD/JPY is an inferior carry vehicle to its peers. Crucially, the differential is on a narrowing trajectory — the BoJ is the only G10 hiker (toward 1.25% by Q4) while the BoC sits at the bottom of its 2.25-3.25% neutral range with no hiking impulse. The carry cushion shrinks every BoJ meeting. The verdict: CAD/JPY is a weak carry trade whose primary appeal is structurally eroding, while its downside convexity is among the worst in G10 due to the double-short positioning and the commodity-vs-haven dynamic.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral rate differential is the dominant factor and it is compressing — bearish for CAD/JPY. CAD out-yields JPY by +125bp at the policy level, but the direction of travel is unambiguously toward narrowing: the BoJ delivered a landmark hike to 1.00% on 16 June (its highest since 1995), while the BoC held at 2.25% with a dovish lean. This is the critical bilateral asymmetry — Japan is the only G10 central bank actively hiking while Canada sits at the bottom of its neutral range with two-sided-to-dovish risk. The +171bp 10Y spread (Canada 3.46% vs Japan ~1.75%) provides a wider term cushion but is also narrowing as JGB yields rise post-YCC. Tactically, the hike is priced and the +125bp carry still nominally favours long CAD/JPY, but with no near-term BoC hike and the next BoJ meeting (31 July) carrying hawkish-guidance risk, the marginal rate news flow leans yen-positive. The pair pays a thin carry to hold while facing a steady stream of differential-compression headlines.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 1-6 months the policy mosaic decisively favours a lower CAD/JPY. The BoJ's normalization is the engine: a hike to 1.25% by Q4 narrows the bilateral gap to +100bp, while the BoC's most probable path is a hold with mild easing risk — both vectors compress the differential from both ends. The 10Y spread should narrow further as JGB yields rise and Canadian growth keeps the BoC capped. For the first time in this cycle, the rate-change direction works against CAD/JPY: the high-yielder is static-to-easing while the low-yielder is hiking. This is the structural unwinding of the very carry trade that lifted the pair. The risk to the bearish view is that even at a narrowing +100bp the carry still nominally pays, so the unwind requires either a vol catalyst or continued BoJ hawkishness to convert into a sustained move.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The growth differential favours JPY over CAD, a bilateral bearish-CAD/JPY input. Canada's economy is at stall speed — Q1 GDP flat 0.0%, March monthly -0.1%, unemployment 6.6% — whereas Japan beat with Q1 GDP +0.5% QoQ (+2.1% annualised), unemployment at 2.5%, and a third straight 5%+ Shunto wage round. On inflation, both are near or below target (Canada core trim/median ~2.0-2.1%; Japan core CPI a soft 1.4%), so neither central bank faces hawkish pressure, but the relative cyclical position clearly favours Japan: Japan is mid-expansion with a normalizing central bank, Canada is stall-speed with a dovish lean. The soft Japanese April CPI tempers the yen story tactically, but on a head-to-head growth basis Canada is the weaker economy, which weighs on CAD/JPY.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically the bilateral inflation-growth story still leans bearish-CAD/JPY but less decisively. Japan's entrenched 5%+ wage cycle and ~2.8% underlying inflation gauge sustain the BoJ's hiking mandate — the structural engine of yen strength — while Canada's durable, target-consistent disinflation removes any BoC hawkishness that could defend CAD. The offset is that Canada's economy is tightly tethered to a still-strong US (Canadian exports ~75% US-bound), so a US-led 2H pickup could lift Canadian growth and partly close the gap. But Japan's wage-price normalization is the more powerful structural shift, and it underwrites the differential compression in Factor 1. Net, the growth/inflation differential supports a gradual CAD/JPY decline.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: CAD/JPY is one of the purest risk-on/risk-off barometers in G10 — a commodity proxy (CAD) versus the world's premier safe-haven and carry funder (JPY). The pair is described as one of the most classic risk-related pairs and very sensitive to risk sentiment. The current risk-on regime (VIX ~16.2, S&P +8.5% YTD, post-Iran-deal calm) is therefore bullish CAD/JPY tactically: haven demand for yen evaporates and carry trades (short yen, long commodity-FX) accumulate, lifting the pair. This is the single factor pulling CAD/JPY higher near-term. The complication is oil: the same de-escalation that calms risk also crushes oil, so CAD's commodity leg loses its bid even as risk-on suppresses the yen — the two channels partly offset within CAD/JPY specifically. On net, with VIX falling and equities firm, the risk channel tilts modestly bullish for the pair, but it is the factor most prone to abrupt reversal given the double-short positioning.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically the risk channel flips to bearish-CAD/JPY because the multi-month base rate almost always includes at least one vol episode, and CAD/JPY is the canonical pair to suffer in it. A carry unwind hits both legs in the same direction: yen rallies (haven + short-cover) while CAD falls (commodity/risk proxy), producing outsized downside. Multiple strategists flag this risk — BCA called the yen carry trade 'a ticking time bomb,' vulnerable to a massive unwind, and commentary notes that when long carry positions are reduced, the flow dynamics can drive rapid declines that spill across FX, and such unwinds rarely remain confined to currency markets. The August 2024 template (USD/JPY 161→141 in three weeks) shows how violently CAD/JPY would fall in such an episode. With the carry book estimated at ~$500bn and positioning at record extremes, the strategic convexity in CAD/JPY is decisively to the downside.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: This is the most nuanced bilateral factor because specs are crowded short BOTH currencies. CAD: leveraged -58.6k, asset managers -73.6k (~6th percentile). JPY: combined -177.9k (~4th percentile), the larger and more dangerous book. On a pure net basis the two shorts partly cancel, muting the directional signal — but the asymmetry matters. The yen short is the bigger coiled spring; a squeeze in JPY (the more extreme position, sitting into a live MoF intervention zone in the USD/JPY leg) would force more yen-buying than the parallel CAD short-cover would generate CAD-buying. Therefore the net contrarian convexity in CAD/JPY points DOWN: the pair is the prime vehicle for a yen squeeze. Tactically, with both shorts still building and risk-on persisting, the positioning can extend (pair drifts in range), but the latent squeeze risk is biased to a sharp CAD/JPY fall.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically the positioning convexity is firmly bearish-CAD/JPY. The record yen short is a multi-quarter accumulation that mirrors the pre-August-2024 setup, and the catalysts to unwind it — BoJ hikes, Fed reprice, MoF intervention, a vol shock — are all live over the horizon. When that unwind comes, CAD/JPY is among the cleanest expressions: it combines a yen short-cover with a commodity-CAD risk-off. The CAD short would partly cover too, but the yen book is roughly 19x larger by spec notional and structurally embedded in ~$500bn of carry, so the net flow is overwhelmingly yen-buying = CAD/JPY lower. Japanese institutional flows add a structural tilt: GPIF and lifers face pressure to lift hedge ratios/repatriate, a slow yen-positive drag. Bilateral retail flows (Uridashi/Toshin) historically favoured higher-yielders like AUD/NZD over CAD, and with the CAD-JPY carry now thin, Japanese retail demand for CAD assets is a fading support.

tactical.contrarian_case: The crowded double-short creates a coiled-spring dynamic uniquely dangerous for CAD/JPY. Because the JPY short (~$500bn carry book, 19x larger by spec notional) dwarfs the CAD short (~$9.5bn), a generalized carry-unwind forces net yen-buying that overwhelms any CAD short-cover, driving the pair sharply lower. strategic.contrarian_case: The strategic contrarian case is that the market treats CAD/JPY as a carry-equilibrium pair while ignoring the deeply asymmetric positioning risk. The JPY short is a multi-quarter accumulation embedded in ~$500bn of structural carry; its unwind, when it comes, will be violent and non-linear. CAD/JPY is the worst vehicle to be long in such an episode — both legs move against the long simultaneously. Meanwhile, Japanese institutional repatriation (GPIF, lifers) and fading Uridashi retail demand for thin-carry CAD assets provide a slow structural drain. The market is pricing none of this; the contrarian short-CAD/JPY position is asymmetrically rewarded.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The oil collapse is doubly bearish for CAD/JPY — the single cleanest bilateral signal. CAD is a petro-currency: WTI fell from ~$95 to ~$75.5 on the 15 June US-Iran deal, stripping CAD's terms-of-trade bid. JPY is the mirror image: Japan imports 99.7% of its oil and 97.7% of its LNG, so the same oil collapse sharply improves Japan's import bill and terms of trade. Applying the commodity direction rules: oil down → CAD weaker (mild-to-moderate negative), JPY stronger (strong positive). Net effect: CAD/JPY falls — the two terms-of-trade vectors reinforce, not offset. This is the textbook commodity-vs-energy-importer dynamic that the April rally rode in reverse. On bilateral trade flows, the IMF DOTS data shows Canada runs a goods deficit with Japan: CAD net goods balance vs JPY: -$5.2bn (CAD exports $10.9bn, imports $16.2bn) — a small structural CAD-negative flow that is dwarfed by the oil channel but reinforces the direction.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: At its heaviest strategic weight (0.16), external balance is decisively bearish-CAD/JPY. Japan runs a structural current-account surplus near +4.8% of GDP, anchored by a record ¥41.6tn primary-income stream from its ~$3.4-3.7tn net creditor position — a permanent yen bid independent of sentiment. Canada runs a modest CA deficit (-0.87%) that widens when oil is low. The bilateral contrast is stark: a structural-surplus, deeply-cheap quote currency versus a deficit, oil-dependent base currency. If oil bases in the $70s-low-$80s, Japan's terms of trade structurally improve while Canada's external accounts stay pressured. The offsets are modest: Canada's Trans Mountain capacity lets it monetize more barrels, and a constructive USMCA review could lift the CAD tail — but neither rivals Japan's structural surplus engine. The net strategic external read strongly favours a lower CAD/JPY.

net_commodity_exposure.base_commodity_profile: CAD is a petro-currency — oil is the dominant commodity driver; WTI/Brent fall directly weakens CAD via terms-of-trade deterioration
net_commodity_exposure.quote_commodity_profile: JPY is an energy-importer currency — Japan imports 99.7% of oil and 97.7% of LNG; oil price falls directly improve Japan's import bill and terms of trade, strengthening the yen
net_commodity_exposure.net_effect_calculation: Step 1: Oil falls (WTI ~$75.5, down ~20% from ~$95). Step 2: Apply direction rule to base — oil↓ → CAD weaker (mild-to-moderate negative for base). Step 3: Apply direction rule to quote — oil↓ → JPY stronger (strong positive for quote). Step 4: Net pair effect — both vectors reinforce in the same direction (base weaker + quote stronger = pair FALLS). Step 5: This is a unique doubly-potent oil channel: unlike most commodity crosses where oil moves one leg, here oil simultaneously hits the base negatively AND benefits the quote positively. The bilateral amplification is approximately 1.5-2x a single-leg commodity cross.

net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_down_10pct: Oil -10% (to ~$68) → CAD terms-of-trade deterioration worsens (CAD-negative) AND Japan's import bill improves further (JPY-positive) → CAD/JPY FALLS; net pair-bearish — amplified bilateral impact, pair likely -2-3 big figures toward 111-112

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Guidance divergence favours JPY. The BoJ has turned demonstrably hawkish — hiking 7-1 even with Governor Ueda hospitalised, with board members consistently signalling further hikes — while the BoC's 10 June guidance was deliberately neutral ('look through' the oil shock, two-sided risk, 'stand ready to respond'). The bilateral contrast is a committed normalizer versus a balanced-to-dovish holder. Both central banks are credible and transparent, so there is no credibility premium either way, but the direction of guidance clearly favours the yen. The next sequenced events matter: the BoC meets 15 July (likely a steady hold with MPR), then the BoJ meets 31 July with an Outlook Report that could refresh hawkish guidance — the BoC sets a neutral tone first, the BoJ a potentially hawkish one second, biasing CAD/JPY lower through the cluster.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: At 0.12 strategic weight, the guidance differential is a meaningful bearish-CAD/JPY input. The BoJ's normalization narrative — exiting NIRP and YCC, hiking to 1.00%, tapering JGB purchases, with consistent hawkish messaging across the board — is durable and increasingly trusted, the first time in a generation BoJ guidance is a yen tailwind. The BoC, by contrast, sits at the bottom of its neutral range signalling it is done easing but with no hiking impulse, a guidance posture that caps CAD without supporting it. The Outlook Report cycle (31 July, 30 October) gives the BoJ repeated chances to cement the hawkish path; the BoC's MPRs (15 July, 28 October) are more likely to confirm a steady hold. The asymmetry — an actively hawkening BoJ versus a static BoC — sustains the differential compression and points the pair lower.

Neither is a 2-4 week catalyst, so this is a quiet, mildly pair-supportive background factor.

But crucially, rising JGB yields also narrow the bilateral spread (yen-positive via Factor 1), so the fiscal channel partly works against itself. Net, a mild pair-positive tilt from Canada's superior credit, tempered by the ambiguity of Japan's normalization-vs-stress dynamic.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically USD funding is a latent two-sided factor for CAD/JPY but currently balanced. The key asymmetry is on the yen leg: if the JPY/USD basis widens materially, Japanese institutions face higher costs to hold USD assets and may repatriate (sell USD, buy yen) — a self-reinforcing yen-positive, pair-negative dynamic. TD notes Japan holds roughly $1.2 trillion in US Treasuries as the largest foreign creditor, and the era of near-zero rates pushing Japanese savings abroad is ending, a structural repatriation tilt that is yen-positive. The CAD leg has no equivalent repatriation trigger (Canadian flows are structurally outward into US assets). With QT over and reserves ample, neither basis-widening is the base case, so the repatriation trigger is dormant — but the structural direction (Japanese capital coming home) is a slow CAD/JPY-negative drift. The dollar-smile is the swing: a soft-USD world helps both legs roughly equally; a US melt-up firms USD against both. Net Neutral with a latent yen-positive (pair-negative) tail.

strategic.rationale: Strategically the bilateral thesis is decisively lower. Here the base and quote views align with unusual force — the CAD strategic view is Neutral but the JPY strategic view is firmly Bullish, and against the cheap yen, CAD/JPY is the cross where Japan's structural strength most directly meets Canada's softness. Guidance (-0.072) adds the BoJ's durable hawkish normalization versus the static BoC. The bilateral-specific amplifier is the REER chasm — CAD/JPY sits ~47% above fair value (96.58 vs 65.70), a classic mean-reversion-lower setup. What is priced: consensus expects gradual yen appreciation but treats CAD/JPY as a carry hold; one survey sees the pair softer at 111.06 year-end 2026 while model-driven forecasters project higher toward 119-120. Our edge is conviction that the differential-compression plus the dual terms-of-trade hit plus the carry-unwind convexity drive the pair toward 108-112. First, the speed of the +125bp differential compression as the BoJ hikes while the BoC is capped — most models price a gradual drift rather than the accelerating compression we see from the unique one-hiker/one-capped dynamic. Second, the dual terms-of-trade impact from oil — rarely modeled as simultaneously helping the JPY-positive and CAD-negative sides, yet this doubly-potent oil channel is the proximate driver. Third, the carry-unwind convexity in this specific commodity-vs-haven cross — linear forecasting models ignore the fat-tailed, asymmetric payoff profile in which both legs move against a CAD/JPY long simultaneously. The bank-research camp broadly agrees on direction but underestimates the magnitude; model-driven forecasters project higher based on carry persistence that we view as structurally eroding.

A dovish tilt in the MPR language is CAD-negative (pair-negative); a confident, balanced hold is mildly CAD-positive. Critically, this sets the tone before the BoJ meets two weeks later — a dovish BoC followed by a hawkish BoJ creates a sequential guidance gap that widens the bilateral asymmetry and pushes CAD/JPY lower through the cluster.

A hawkish hold sustains the carry/risk-on tape (pair-positive by suppressing yen and supporting CAD through the risk channel); any dovish tilt triggers a yen-positive carry-unwind impulse (pair-negative). The bilateral transmission runs through the dollar-smile and risk channel: a dovish Fed weakens the dollar broadly, loosens financial conditions, but may also reprice the global carry trade — the yen leg's sensitivity to Fed re-pricing is higher than the CAD leg's, so a dovish surprise is net pair-negative.

The key JPY catalyst and the single most important bilateral event for the pair. Ueda expected back; the Outlook Report provides a CPI-path revision and any Q4-hike signal. Hawkish guidance or an explicit 1.25% path squeezes the record yen short (-177,920 contracts), driving CAD/JPY sharply lower via the quote leg. The bilateral transmission is direct: BoJ hawkishness compresses the +125bp differential, erodes the carry cushion, and forces yen short-cover — all three channels point to a lower pair. A July hold with hawkish guidance (the base case) still delivers pair-negative differential-compression messaging.

A clean expiry removes a CAD headwind (CAD-positive, pair-positive) by eliminating the tariff overhang that has suppressed CAD risk premium; a renewal reinstates the tariff drag (pair-negative). This is a CAD-leg-specific event with no bilateral Japan component. The transmission runs through CAD risk premium: the surcharge has been one of the factors keeping CAD depressed versus fundamentals, so its removal could produce a sharp CAD relief rally and lift the pair 1-2 figures.

Follows a strong Q1 (+2.1% annualised). A solid Q2 print supports the BoJ normalization case, keeping the hiking path intact (yen-positive, pair-negative); a miss raises pause risk (pair-positive as the BoJ Q4 hike gets repriced out). The bilateral importance is that Japan's growth trajectory is the fundamental underpinning for the BoJ's hiking mandate — without growth confirmation, the normalization story loses credibility and the bearish-CAD/JPY strategic case is weakened.

catalysts.event: BoJ September decision (following BoC September meeting ~2 Sep)
catalysts.rationale: Sequenced central-bank meetings: a steady-dovish BoC against a hawkish-hold or hiking BoJ widens the policy-direction gap, compressing the bilateral differential and pressuring CAD/JPY lower. The bilateral transmission is the same as the July cluster but with less novelty — by September the market may have already begun repricing, so the incremental CAD/JPY move may be smaller unless the BoJ surprises with a September hike rather than waiting for October.

catalysts.event: BoJ decision + Outlook Report (likely Q4 hike to 1.25%)
catalysts.rationale: The most likely meeting for the 25bp hike to 1.25%, narrowing the bilateral differential to ~+100bp and cementing the strategic short-CAD/JPY thesis. The Outlook CPI revision is decisive — if the BoJ raises its FY2026/27 inflation forecasts, it opens the door to further hikes beyond 1.25%, which would accelerate the differential compression. The bilateral transmission is maximum-impact: every 25bp BoJ hike erodes the carry cushion (from +125bp to +100bp), forces yen short-cover in the record short, and mechanically drives CAD/JPY lower via both the rate differential and the positioning channel.

With the yen near multi-decade lows and a record speculative short, renewed MoF intervention is a live recurring catalyst that bids the yen broadly — mechanically dragging CAD/JPY lower as the JPY leg strengthens. Japan intervened in May (~$35bn) with ~162 cited as the tolerance ceiling in USD/JPY. The bilateral transmission is one-sided and immediate: yen-buying intervention strengthens JPY regardless of CAD fundamentals, so CAD/JPY falls purely through the quote leg. This is a one-sided downside catalyst with no CAD-positive offset — the market's tendency to fade intervention creates recurring entry opportunities for the bearish pair view.

The dual terms-of-trade driver and the cleanest bilateral CAD/JPY signal. Oil sub-$80 weakens CAD (petro-currency loses its bid) AND simultaneously strengthens JPY (Japan's import bill improves as the world's largest energy importer). The bilateral transmission is doubly potent — unlike most commodity crosses where oil moves one leg, here oil simultaneously impacts the base negatively and the quote positively, amplifying the pair move by roughly 1.5-2x. A sustained oil rebound above $90 is the single most dangerous invalidator of the bearish thesis, as it would reverse both legs simultaneously.

invalidators.condition: WTI crude oil rebounds and sustains above $90/bbl on stalled Iran-deal implementation or renewed Middle East escalation
invalidators.mechanism: The dual terms-of-trade dynamic reverses — CAD regains its petro-bid while Japan's import bill worsens, the exact April-rally driver that lifted the pair from 105 to 117.49. Both legs flip: CAD strengthens (higher oil improves Canada's ToT and external balance) while JPY weakens (higher energy costs erode Japan's surplus and worsen the current account). The primary bearish thesis — that oil is doubly negative for CAD/JPY — is fully reversed.

invalidators.condition: Bilateral 2Y/policy differential WIDENS back above +175bp (BoJ pauses while BoC holds, or a hawkish BoC surprise)
invalidators.mechanism: The restored carry cushion revives the long-carry appeal and removes the differential-compression thesis — the core driver of the bearish CAD/JPY call. A wider differential means the carry trade economics improve (higher carry-to-vol), incentivising fresh long CAD/JPY positions and short-covering of existing yen longs. The primary engine of the bearish thesis (BoJ hiking, BoC capped) is invalidated.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: BoC and BoJ policy rates; Canada-Japan 2Y yield spread

invalidators.condition: Risk-on deepens with VIX sustained below 14 and AUD/JPY pushing fresh multi-decade highs
invalidators.mechanism: Deep carry-on conditions suppress the yen across all crosses and reignite commodity-FX carry demand, overwhelming the differential compression. In this scenario, the yen's safe-haven premium evaporates entirely, the carry book expands rather than unwinds, and the risk channel (Factor 3) overwhelms all other bearish factors. CAD benefits doubly — as a commodity and a risk proxy.

invalidators.condition: BoJ explicitly signals a pause in normalization (e.g., Japan core CPI falls below 1.0% forcing a dovish Outlook)
invalidators.mechanism: The rate-path engine of the bearish thesis stalls and the carry short stabilizes, removing the primary differential-compression driver. Without a hiking BoJ, the +125bp carry persists indefinitely, the yen short is no longer a coiled spring, and CAD/JPY becomes a range-trade rather than a directional short. The bearish conviction drops to 1 and the strategic target is revised toward 113-116.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: BoJ Outlook Report language; Japan core CPI

invalidators.condition: CAD/JPY breaks and holds above 117.50 (the 30 April cycle high) on combined oil-rebound and risk-on
invalidators.mechanism: The topping structure is invalidated and trend-followers re-engage long, flipping the technical regime from topping-and-rolling to breakout-and-trending. A break of the cycle high confirms that the May-June selloff was a correction rather than a reversal, that carry demand has overwhelmed the differential-compression thesis, and that CAD's petro-bid has returned. Systematic strategies and CTA momentum would pile in above 117.50.

signposts.confirms_if: Compresses toward or below +100bp (BoJ hikes to 1.25%, BoC holds at 2.25%)
signposts.invalidates_if: Widens back above +175bp (BoJ pause or BoC hawkish surprise)
signposts.bilateral_importance: This is the carry differential that IS the pair's structural support; its compression is the core bearish-CAD/JPY driver and matters more here than either currency's absolute rate path — a 25bp BoJ hike that moves the differential from +125bp to +100bp is a more powerful CAD/JPY signal than any macro data print.
signposts.source: Bank of Canada rate announcements / Bank of Japan rate announcements; Canada-Japan 2Y yield spread via Bloomberg or Refinitiv

signposts.invalidates_if: Sustained above $90 (CAD's petro-bid returns, Japan's import bill worsens)
signposts.bilateral_importance: Uniquely doubly potent for CAD/JPY — the only major G10 cross where one commodity simultaneously hits the base negatively (CAD petro-currency) and benefits the quote positively (JPY energy-importer); oil is therefore the cleanest and most direct single signal for this pair, with each $10 move in WTI estimated to move CAD/JPY by approximately 1-2 big figures in the same direction.

signposts.confirms_if: Spikes above 25 (forces carry unwind, bids yen, hits CAD commodity exposure simultaneously)
signposts.invalidates_if: Sustains below 14 (deep risk-on reignites the carry and suppresses the yen across all crosses)
signposts.bilateral_importance: CAD/JPY is among the highest-risk-beta crosses in G10 (commodity vs haven); a vol spike moves both legs in the same downward direction — yen rallies as a haven while CAD falls as a risk/commodity proxy — amplifying CAD/JPY downside far more than for low-beta pairs. The $500bn yen carry book means VIX 25 is not just a sentiment signal but a forced-liquidation trigger.

signposts.confirms_if: Begins unwinding (becomes less short, trending toward -100k) as CAD/JPY falls — confirming the squeeze is underway
signposts.invalidates_if: Extends beyond -200k while CAD/JPY grinds higher, confirming the carry trade has further room to run
signposts.bilateral_importance: The yen short is the larger, more dangerous of the two crowded shorts in this pair; its unwind dominates the pair's convexity (yen-buying overwhelms the parallel CAD short-cover, since the yen book is ~19x larger by spec notional), making CAD/JPY the prime carry-unwind vehicle — the weekly CFTC report is the earliest systematic signal of whether the carry regime is ending.

signposts.confirms_if: Decisive daily close below 112.00 with rising volume (range floor breaks, opens 108-110 technical target)
signposts.invalidates_if: Rallies and holds above 115.40 (reclaims the range top and the MA cluster, invalidating the topping structure)
signposts.bilateral_importance: The 112.00 level is the repeatedly-defended structural floor for THIS pair specifically ('Loonie bulls have repeatedly defended the 112.00 area'); a break flips the multi-month technical regime and invites trend-following shorts, a self-reinforcing pair-level catalyst not captured by either standalone currency's chart — it is CAD/JPY-specific and therefore the purest bilateral confirmation signal.

signposts.confirms_if: Re-accelerates above 2.0%, supporting BoJ hiking path and cementing Q4 hike expectation (yen-positive, pair-negative)
signposts.invalidates_if: Falls below 1.0%, forcing BoJ to signal a pause in normalization (pair-positive)
signposts.bilateral_importance: Japan's core CPI trajectory is the fundamental driver of the BoJ's hiking mandate — the engine of differential compression that is the primary bearish-CAD/JPY force. Unlike standalone JPY analysis where CPI matters for yen broadly, in CAD/JPY the CPI matters specifically because it determines how fast the +125bp bilateral carry differential compresses toward +100bp and beyond.

assumptions: Spot and price-derived metrics (113.81 close, performance figures, 52-week range 101.25-117.49, moving averages 50d 115.08 / 100d 115.38 / 200d 114.53) are taken from the provided Twelve Data OHLC as the source of truth. The CAD dossier's CAD/JPY reference of 114.63 reflects an earlier 17 June snapshot; the 19 June close of 113.81 is used per the price data.

Bilateral carry/differential computed directly from the two dossiers' policy rates (BoC 2.25%, BoJ 1.00% = +125bp) and 10Y yields (Canada 3.46%, Japan ~1.75% = +171bp). A minor cross-dossier discrepancy in the BoJ rate (CAD dossier cites BoJ 1.00% / +125bp; JPY dossier confirms the 16 June hike to 1.00%) was resolved by using 1.00%, giving +125bp bilateral gap.
Bilateral trade data taken directly from the injected IMF DOTS block (CAD net goods balance -$5.2bn vs Japan; CAD exports $10.9bn, imports $16.2bn); not re-estimated from web sources.

Pair volatility (~9-11% 1M IV) is inferred by blending the two legs' implied vols (CAD ~6.8%, JPY ~9.0%) with imperfect correlation, as no direct CAD/JPY option print was available; treated as structural context and directional guidance only.
Support and resistance levels are sourced from web technical analysis (TradingView, FXStreet, ActionForex, Babypips, FX Daily Report); trend regime and moving average levels are from the price data.

Forward points are estimated from the bilateral rate differential using covered-interest-parity approximation (not a live forward feed); directional accuracy is high but precise pip values are approximations.

Weight adjustments (Factor 3 boosted to 0.13/0.08, Factors 1 trimmed to 0.22/0.23, Factor 5 tactical lifted to 0.07, Factor 10 trimmed to 0.09/0.10) reflect the pair's pronounced risk-barometer character and non-USD-cross status; all adjustments are within ±0.03 of defaults and sum to 1.00 at each horizon.

Commodity netting for oil follows the framework's direction rules: oil↓ → CAD weak (mild-to-moderate negative for base), JPY strong (strong positive for quote) → net pair falls. This rule is applied consistently and both dossiers confirm the direction.

The REER comparison (Canada 96.58 vs Japan 65.70, implying ~47% CAD overvaluation vs JPY) is derived from the two dossiers' individual REER data points and combined bilaterally; this is a rough ratio and not a precise bilateral REER calculation.

The yen carry book estimate (~$500bn) is from web research (BCA/Bloomberg sources) and is a widely cited approximation; the actual size is unobservable. The CAD spec notional (~$9.5bn) is estimated from CFTC contract sizes and current net positioning.
